title: The Fourth Circuit Says Border Agents Can Search Your Phone By Hand, No Suspicion Required
excerpt: The court ruled that a lower standard applies to manual searches, allowing the government to conduct extraordinarily invasive electronic device searches without any suspicion of wrongdoing, simply because the border officer chooses to search by hand rather than with a forensic tool.
content_html:
Legal intern Suzanne Castillo was the principal author of this post.

The Fourth Circuit issued a disappointing opinion in U.S. v. Belmonte Cardozo, a case in which EFF filed an amicus brief, alongside the national ACLU, its Maryland, North Carolina, South Carolina, and Virginia affiliates, and the National Association of Criminal Defense Lawyers (NACDL).

We argued that electronic device searches at the border should require a warrant based on probable cause, but at minimum, regardless of whether an officer searches by hand or with forensic software that plugs into a device and downloads its entire contents for search, the same Fourth Amendment standard should apply to all device searches at the border.

Unfortunately, the court rejected that argument and ruled that a lower standard applies to manual searches, allowing the government to conduct extraordinarily invasive electronic device searches without any suspicion of wrongdoing, simply because the border officer chooses to search by hand rather than with a forensic tool.

The Border Search Exception Meets Your Phone

The Fourth Amendment requires that government searches of persons or property be reasonable, which usually means obtaining a warrant based on probable cause from a judge.

But a warrantless search can still be reasonable if it falls within an exception to the warrant requirement, including the exception that allows officers to search your belongings at the border. The border search exception allows warrantless searches of persons or property crossing the U.S. border, including the functional equivalent of the border such as international airports, given the government’s interests in controlling who and what may enter the country.

Historically, courts have categorized border searches of luggage, vehicles, and personal effects as “routine” and thus reasonable even if conducted without any suspicion that the traveler has engaged in wrongdoing; courts have also held that more invasive “nonroutine” searches, such as certain body searches and searches that damage property, require reasonable suspicion.

But a person’s privacy interests in the personal data on a phone or laptop are extraordinarily different than their limited privacy interests in the contents of their suitcase.

The Supreme Court addressed cell phone privacy in Riley v. California (2014), holding that the search-incident-to-arrest exception to the warrant requirement did not apply to cell phones, thereby generally requiring a warrant for phone searches, at least at the interior of the country. The court recognized the unprecedented privacy interests people have in their cell phones and how even brief manual searches can reveal the “sum of an individual’s private life,” including our political affiliations, religious beliefs, sexuality, and more. Accordingly, the Supreme Court held that because electronic device searches bear “little resemblance” to searches of bags or physical containers, they should be evaluated differently.

Following Riley, the Fourth Circuit considered two border device search cases involving forensic searches, in which border officers used external software to extract and analyze a device’s data.

In U.S. v. Kolsuz (2018), the Fourth Circuit held that a forensic search of a cell phone at the border “must be considered a nonroutine border search, requiring some measure of individualized suspicion” of a transnational offense, but the court declined to decide whether the standard is only reasonable suspicion or instead a probable cause warrant.

Then in U.S. v. Aigbekaen (2019), the Fourth Circuit held that a forensic device search at the border in support of a purely domestic law enforcement investigation requires a warrant. The court also reiterated the general Kolsuz rule for a forensic border-related device search: the “Government must have individualized suspicion of an offense that bears some nexus to the border search exception's purposes of protecting national security, collecting duties, blocking the entry of unwanted persons, or disrupting efforts to export or import contraband.”

In Belmonte Cardozo, manual searches were finally before the court.

A Disappointing Decision

Jose Belmonte Cardozo was already on the U.S. government’s radar when he traveled from Bolivia to the U.S. and was met by a U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) officer at Washington Dulles International Airport. The officer manually searched his cell phone and found child sexual abuse material (CSAM), considered “digital contraband,” leading to Belmonte Cardozo’s arrest and criminal prosecution.

At issue on appeal was what standard should apply to manual device searches at the border. The Fourth Circuit held that, unlike forensic searches, manual searches are “routine” and thus reasonable under the Fourth Amendment without a warrant or individualized suspicion.

The court’s holding hinged on four differences between manual and forensic searches: (1) in a manual search, a person does the searching, not a machine; (2) a manual search’s breadth depends on the officer’s time and energy, while forensic searches are comprehensive; (3) manual searches reveal only what a user can typically access, while forensic searches can uncover deleted files, cached fragments, metadata, and more; and (4) manual searches are subject to an officer’s fading memory or imperfect notes, while forensic searches create a permanent copy.

But in identifying these technical differences, the court never explains why they justify a lower standard for manual searches.

The Fourth Circuit’s holding is problematic because, as we argued in our amicus brief, manual searches reach the same categories of data as forensic searches—data that can reveal highly personal aspects of our identities and our lives. It does not matter if a search is conducted by an agent’s thumbs or by software: the end result is equally as invasive, therefore all device searches should fall under the warrant requirement, or at least the same Fourth Amendment standard.

The court repeatedly emphasized that the search here lasted only two minutes, suggesting that the time-limited search was not privacy-invasive. But an individual’s privacy interests in their personal data don’t change based on how their phone is searched or how long. Scrolling for two minutes through someone’s personal text messages or photos is an invasion of privacy that may reveal intimate details about the person even in that short period of time.

Moreover, as devices’ native search functions improve, manual searches can surface personal information in seconds through keyword searches, even for photos, where it might have taken an hour of scrolling to find the same information, further showing that a time-limited search is not necessarily less privacy-invasive. What matters is not the breadth of the search itself, but the unprecedented (and growing) breadth of data on our phones.

A Silver Lining

There’s one silver lining: by relying on the fact that the search lasted two minutes, the Fourth Circuit left open the possibility that lengthier manual searches could trigger heightened suspicion requirements. But until a clear line is drawn, border officers within the Fourth Circuit’s jurisdiction can use manual searches to sidestep heightened Fourth Amendment standards that would otherwise apply. In the meantime, EFF will keep fighting against extraordinarily invasive warrantless, suspicionless device earches at the border, and for robust privacy standards to protect our most personal data.

sentences.en: Legal intern Suzanne Castillo was the principal author of this post.
sentences.zh: 法律实习生 Suzanne Castillo 是这篇文章的主要作者。

blocks.html_en: The Fourth Circuit issued a disappointing opinion in U.S. v. Belmonte Cardozo, a case in which EFF filed an amicus brief, alongside the national ACLU, its Maryland, North Carolina, South Carolina, and Virginia affiliates, and the National Association of Criminal Defense Lawyers (NACDL).

sentences.en: The Fourth Circuit issued a disappointing opinion in U.S. v.
sentences.zh: 第四巡回法院在 U.S. v. 案中发表了令人失望的意见。

sentences.en: Belmonte Cardozo, a case in which EFF filed an amicus brief, alongside the national ACLU, its Maryland, North Carolina, South Carolina, and Virginia affiliates, and the National Association of Criminal Defense Lawyers (NACDL).
sentences.zh: 在 Belmonte Cardozo 案中，EFF 与美国公民自由联盟、其马里兰州、北卡罗来纳州、南卡罗来纳州和弗吉尼亚州的附属机构以及全国刑事辩护律师协会 (NACDL) 一起提交了一份非当事人意见陈述。

blocks.html_en: We argued that electronic device searches at the border should require a warrant based on probable cause, but at minimum, regardless of whether an officer searches by hand or with forensic software that plugs into a device and downloads its entire contents for search, the same Fourth Amendment standard should apply to all device searches at the border.

sentences.en: We argued that electronic device searches at the border should require a warrant based on probable cause, but at minimum, regardless of whether an officer searches by hand or with forensic software that plugs into a device and downloads its entire contents for search, the same Fourth Amendment standard should apply to all device searches at the border.
sentences.zh: 我们认为，边境的电子设备搜查应该需要基于可能原因的搜查令，但至少，无论官员是手动搜查还是使用插入设备并下载其全部内容进行搜查的取证软件进行搜查，相同的第四修正案标准都应适用于边境的所有设备搜查。

blocks.html_en: Unfortunately, the court rejected that argument and ruled that a lower standard applies to manual searches, allowing the government to conduct extraordinarily invasive electronic device searches without any suspicion of wrongdoing, simply because the border officer chooses to search by hand rather than with a forensic tool.

sentences.en: Unfortunately, the court rejected that argument and ruled that a lower standard applies to manual searches, allowing the government to conduct extraordinarily invasive electronic device searches without any suspicion of wrongdoing, simply because the border officer chooses to search by hand rather than with a forensic tool.
sentences.zh: 不幸的是，法院驳回了这一论点，并裁定手动搜查适用较低的标准，从而允许政府在不怀疑有任何不当行为的情况下进行极具侵入性的电子设备搜查，仅仅是因为边境官员选择手动搜查而不是使用法医工具。

blocks.html_en: The Border Search Exception Meets Your Phone

sentences.en: The Border Search Exception Meets Your Phone
sentences.zh: 边境搜查例外与您的手机相遇

blocks.html_en: The Fourth Amendment requires that government searches of persons or property be reasonable, which usually means obtaining a warrant based on probable cause from a judge.

sentences.en: The Fourth Amendment requires that government searches of persons or property be reasonable, which usually means obtaining a warrant based on probable cause from a judge.
sentences.zh: 第四修正案要求政府对人员或财产的搜查是合理的，这通常意味着从法官那里获得基于可能原因的搜查令。

blocks.html_en: But a warrantless search can still be reasonable if it falls within an exception to the warrant requirement, including the exception that allows officers to search your belongings at the border. The border search exception allows warrantless searches of persons or property crossing the U.S. border, including the functional equivalent of the border such as international airports, given the government’s interests in controlling who and what may enter the country.

sentences.en: But a warrantless search can still be reasonable if it falls within an exception to the warrant requirement, including the exception that allows officers to search your belongings at the border.
sentences.zh: 但是，如果属于搜查令要求的例外情况，包括允许官员在边境搜查您的物品的例外情况，则无证搜查仍然是合理的。

sentences.en: The border search exception allows warrantless searches of persons or property crossing the U.S. border, including the functional equivalent of the border such as international airports, given the government’s interests in controlling who and what may enter the country.
sentences.zh: 考虑到政府在控制谁和什么可以进入该国方面的利益，边境搜查例外允许在无授权的情况下搜查穿越美国边境的人员或财产，包括国际机场等边境的功能等同物。

blocks.html_en: Historically, courts have categorized border searches of luggage, vehicles, and personal effects as “routine” and thus reasonable even if conducted without any suspicion that the traveler has engaged in wrongdoing; courts have also held that more invasive “nonroutine” searches, such as certain body searches and searches that damage property, require reasonable suspicion.

sentences.en: Historically, courts have categorized border searches of luggage, vehicles, and personal effects as “routine” and thus reasonable even if conducted without any suspicion that the traveler has engaged in wrongdoing; courts have also held that more invasive “nonroutine” searches, such as certain body searches and searches that damage property, require reasonable suspicion.
sentences.zh: 从历史上看，法院将行李、车辆和个人物品的边境搜查归为“例行公事”，因此即使在没有怀疑旅行者有不当行为的情况下进行，也是合理的；法院还认为，更具侵入性的“非常规”搜查，例如某些身体搜查和损坏财产的搜查，需要合理怀疑。

blocks.html_en: But a person’s privacy interests in the personal data on a phone or laptop are extraordinarily different than their limited privacy interests in the contents of their suitcase.

sentences.en: But a person’s privacy interests in the personal data on a phone or laptop are extraordinarily different than their limited privacy interests in the contents of their suitcase.
sentences.zh: 但一个人对手机或笔记本电脑上的个人数据的隐私利益与他们对手提箱内容的有限隐私利益截然不同。

blocks.html_en: The Supreme Court addressed cell phone privacy in Riley v. California (2014), holding that the search-incident-to-arrest exception to the warrant requirement did not apply to cell phones, thereby generally requiring a warrant for phone searches, at least at the interior of the country. The court recognized the unprecedented privacy interests people have in their cell phones and how even brief manual searches can reveal the “sum of an individual’s private life,” including our political affiliations, religious beliefs, sexuality, and more. Accordingly, the Supreme Court held that because electronic device searches bear “little resemblance” to searches of bags or physical containers, they should be evaluated differently.

sentences.zh: 最高法院在 Riley v. 案中讨论了手机隐私问题。

sentences.en: California (2014), holding that the search-incident-to-arrest exception to the warrant requirement did not apply to cell phones, thereby generally requiring a warrant for phone searches, at least at the interior of the country.

sentences.en: The court recognized the unprecedented privacy interests people have in their cell phones and how even brief manual searches can reveal the “sum of an individual’s private life,” including our political affiliations, religious beliefs, sexuality, and more.
sentences.zh: 法院认识到人们在手机中拥有前所未有的隐私利益，以及即使是简短的手动搜索也可以揭示“个人私生活的全部内容”，包括我们的政治立场、宗教信仰、性取向等。

sentences.en: Accordingly, the Supreme Court held that because electronic device searches bear “little resemblance” to searches of bags or physical containers, they should be evaluated differently.
sentences.zh: 因此，最高法院认为，由于电子设备搜查与袋子或物理容器的搜查“几乎没有相似之处”，因此应对它们进行不同的评估。

blocks.html_en: Following Riley, the Fourth Circuit considered two border device search cases involving forensic searches, in which border officers used external software to extract and analyze a device’s data.

sentences.en: Following Riley, the Fourth Circuit considered two border device search cases involving forensic searches, in which border officers used external software to extract and analyze a device’s data.
sentences.zh: 继莱利之后，第四巡回法院考虑了两个涉及法医搜索的边境设备搜查案件，其中边境官员使用外部软件提取和分析设备的数据。

Kolsuz (2018), the Fourth Circuit held that a forensic search of a cell phone at the border “must be considered a nonroutine border search, requiring some measure of individualized suspicion” of a transnational offense, but the court declined to decide whether the standard is only reasonable suspicion or instead a probable cause warrant.

sentences.en: Kolsuz (2018), the Fourth Circuit held that a forensic search of a cell phone at the border “must be considered a nonroutine border search, requiring some measure of individualized suspicion” of a transnational offense, but the court declined to decide whether the standard is only reasonable suspicion or instead a probable cause warrant.
sentences.zh: Kolsuz (2018)，第四巡回法院认为，在边境对手机进行法证搜查“必须被视为非常规边境搜查，需要对跨国犯罪采取某种程度的个人怀疑”，但法院拒绝决定该标准是否只是合理怀疑，还是合理怀疑。

sentences.zh: 然后在美国诉。

sentences.en: Aigbekaen (2019), the Fourth Circuit held that a forensic device search at the border in support of a purely domestic law enforcement investigation requires a warrant.
sentences.zh: Aigbekaen (2019)，第四巡回法院认为，为支持纯粹的国内执法调查而在边境进行法证设备搜查需要搜查令。

sentences.en: The court also reiterated the general Kolsuz rule for a forensic border-related device search: the “Government must have individualized suspicion of an offense that bears some nexus to the border search exception's purposes of protecting national security, collecting duties, blocking the entry of unwanted persons, or disrupting efforts to export or import contraband.”
sentences.zh: 法院还重申了科尔苏兹关于边境相关设备法医搜查的一般规则：“政府必须对与边境搜查例外的保护国家安全、征收关税、阻止不受欢迎人员入境或扰乱进出口违禁品的目的有某种联系的犯罪行为进行个别怀疑。”

blocks.html_en: In Belmonte Cardozo, manual searches were finally before the court.

sentences.en: In Belmonte Cardozo, manual searches were finally before the court.
sentences.zh: 在贝尔蒙特卡多佐，人工搜查终于在法庭上进行。

blocks.html_en: A Disappointing Decision

sentences.en: A Disappointing Decision
sentences.zh: 令人失望的决定

blocks.html_en: Jose Belmonte Cardozo was already on the U.S. government’s radar when he traveled from Bolivia to the U.S. and was met by a U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) officer at Washington Dulles International Airport. The officer manually searched his cell phone and found child sexual abuse material (CSAM), considered “digital contraband,” leading to Belmonte Cardozo’s arrest and criminal prosecution.

sentences.en: Jose Belmonte Cardozo was already on the U.S. government’s radar when he traveled from Bolivia to the U.S. and was met by a U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) officer at Washington Dulles International Airport.
sentences.zh: 当何塞·贝尔蒙特·卡多佐从玻利维亚前往美国时，他已经受到美国政府的关注，并在华盛顿杜勒斯国际机场受到了美国海关和边境保护局 (CBP) 官员的接见。

sentences.en: The officer manually searched his cell phone and found child sexual abuse material (CSAM), considered “digital contraband,” leading to Belmonte Cardozo’s arrest and criminal prosecution.
sentences.zh: 该警官手动搜查了他的手机，发现了被视为“数字违禁品”的儿童性虐待材料 (CSAM)，导致贝尔蒙特·卡多佐被捕并受到刑事起诉。

blocks.html_en: At issue on appeal was what standard should apply to manual device searches at the border. The Fourth Circuit held that, unlike forensic searches, manual searches are “routine” and thus reasonable under the Fourth Amendment without a warrant or individualized suspicion.

sentences.en: At issue on appeal was what standard should apply to manual device searches at the border.
sentences.zh: 上诉的争议焦点是边境手动设备搜查应适用什么标准。

sentences.en: The Fourth Circuit held that, unlike forensic searches, manual searches are “routine” and thus reasonable under the Fourth Amendment without a warrant or individualized suspicion.
sentences.zh: 第四巡回法院认为，与法医搜查不同，人工搜查是“例行公事”，因此根据第四修正案是合理的，无需搜查令或个人怀疑。

blocks.html_en: The court’s holding hinged on four differences between manual and forensic searches: (1) in a manual search, a person does the searching, not a machine; (2) a manual search’s breadth depends on the officer’s time and energy, while forensic searches are comprehensive; (3) manual searches reveal only what a user can typically access, while forensic searches can uncover deleted files, cached fragments, metadata, and more; and (4) manual searches are subject to an officer’s fading memory or imperfect notes, while forensic searches create a permanent copy.

sentences.en: The court’s holding hinged on four differences between manual and forensic searches: (1) in a manual search, a person does the searching, not a machine; (2) a manual search’s breadth depends on the officer’s time and energy, while forensic searches are comprehensive; (3) manual searches reveal only what a user can typically access, while forensic searches can uncover deleted files, cached fragments, metadata, and more; and (4) manual searches are subject to an officer’s fading memory or imperfect notes, while forensic searches create a permanent copy.
sentences.zh: 法院的判决取决于手动搜查和法医搜查之间的四个区别：（1）在手动搜查中，是人进行搜索，而不是机器； (2) 人工搜查的广度取决于官员的时间和精力，而法医搜查则涉及全面； (3) 手动搜索仅揭示用户通常可以访问的内容，而取证搜索可以发现已删除的文件、缓存的片段、元数据等； (4) 手动搜查会受到警官记忆衰退或记录不完善的影响，而法医搜查会创建永久副本。

blocks.html_en: But in identifying these technical differences, the court never explains why they justify a lower standard for manual searches.

sentences.en: But in identifying these technical differences, the court never explains why they justify a lower standard for manual searches.
sentences.zh: 但在识别这些技术差异时，法院从未解释为什么他们证明较低的手动搜索标准是合理的。

blocks.html_en: The Fourth Circuit’s holding is problematic because, as we argued in our amicus brief, manual searches reach the same categories of data as forensic searches—data that can reveal highly personal aspects of our identities and our lives. It does not matter if a search is conducted by an agent’s thumbs or by software: the end result is equally as invasive, therefore all device searches should fall under the warrant requirement, or at least the same Fourth Amendment standard.

sentences.en: The Fourth Circuit’s holding is problematic because, as we argued in our amicus brief, manual searches reach the same categories of data as forensic searches—data that can reveal highly personal aspects of our identities and our lives.
sentences.zh: 第四巡回法院的判决是有问题的，因为正如我们在法庭之友陈述中所说，手动搜索所涉及的数据类别与法医搜索相同，这些数据可以揭示我们身份和生活的高度个人化方面。

sentences.en: It does not matter if a search is conducted by an agent’s thumbs or by software: the end result is equally as invasive, therefore all device searches should fall under the warrant requirement, or at least the same Fourth Amendment standard.
sentences.zh: 搜查是通过特工的拇指还是通过软件进行并不重要：最终结果同样具有侵入性，因此所有设备搜查都应符合搜查令要求，或至少符合第四修正案的标准。

blocks.html_en: The court repeatedly emphasized that the search here lasted only two minutes, suggesting that the time-limited search was not privacy-invasive. But an individual’s privacy interests in their personal data don’t change based on how their phone is searched or how long. Scrolling for two minutes through someone’s personal text messages or photos is an invasion of privacy that may reveal intimate details about the person even in that short period of time.

sentences.en: The court repeatedly emphasized that the search here lasted only two minutes, suggesting that the time-limited search was not privacy-invasive.
sentences.zh: 法院反复强调，这里的搜查只持续了两分钟，表明限时搜查并不侵犯隐私。

sentences.en: But an individual’s privacy interests in their personal data don’t change based on how their phone is searched or how long.
sentences.zh: 但个人对其个人数据的隐私利益不会根据手机被搜索的方式或时间长短而改变。

sentences.en: Scrolling for two minutes through someone’s personal text messages or photos is an invasion of privacy that may reveal intimate details about the person even in that short period of time.
sentences.zh: 滚动浏览某人的个人短信或照片两分钟是对隐私的侵犯，即使在这么短的时间内也可能会泄露该人的私密细节。

blocks.html_en: Moreover, as devices’ native search functions improve, manual searches can surface personal information in seconds through keyword searches, even for photos, where it might have taken an hour of scrolling to find the same information, further showing that a time-limited search is not necessarily less privacy-invasive. What matters is not the breadth of the search itself, but the unprecedented (and growing) breadth of data on our phones.

sentences.en: Moreover, as devices’ native search functions improve, manual searches can surface personal information in seconds through keyword searches, even for photos, where it might have taken an hour of scrolling to find the same information, further showing that a time-limited search is not necessarily less privacy-invasive.
sentences.zh: 此外，随着设备原生搜索功能的改进，手动搜索可以通过关键字搜索在几秒钟内显示个人信息，即使是照片，可能需要滚动一个小时才能找到相同的信息，这进一步表明限时搜索不一定会减少对隐私的侵犯。

sentences.en: What matters is not the breadth of the search itself, but the unprecedented (and growing) breadth of data on our phones.
sentences.zh: 重要的不是搜索本身的广度，而是我们手机上前所未有的（并且不断增长的）数据广度。

blocks.html_en: A Silver Lining

sentences.en: A Silver Lining
sentences.zh: 一线希望

blocks.html_en: There’s one silver lining: by relying on the fact that the search lasted two minutes, the Fourth Circuit left open the possibility that lengthier manual searches could trigger heightened suspicion requirements. But until a clear line is drawn, border officers within the Fourth Circuit’s jurisdiction can use manual searches to sidestep heightened Fourth Amendment standards that would otherwise apply. In the meantime, EFF will keep fighting against extraordinarily invasive warrantless, suspicionless device earches at the border, and for robust privacy standards to protect our most personal data.

sentences.en: There’s one silver lining: by relying on the fact that the search lasted two minutes, the Fourth Circuit left open the possibility that lengthier manual searches could trigger heightened suspicion requirements.
sentences.zh: 还有一线希望：第四巡回法院依靠搜查持续了两分钟的事实，留下了一种可能性，即更长的手动搜查可能会引发更高的怀疑要求。

sentences.en: But until a clear line is drawn, border officers within the Fourth Circuit’s jurisdiction can use manual searches to sidestep heightened Fourth Amendment standards that would otherwise apply.
sentences.zh: 但在划定明确界限之前，第四巡回法院管辖范围内的边境官员可以使用手动搜查来避开本来适用的更高的第四修正案标准。

sentences.en: In the meantime, EFF will keep fighting against extraordinarily invasive warrantless, suspicionless device earches at the border, and for robust privacy standards to protect our most personal data.
sentences.zh: 与此同时，EFF 将继续打击边境上未经授权、毫无嫌疑的极具侵入性的设备窃听行为，并制定严格的隐私标准来保护我们最个人的数据。

content_html_zh:
法律实习生 Suzanne Castillo 是这篇文章的主要作者。

第四巡回法院在 U.S. v. 案中发表了令人失望的意见。 在 Belmonte Cardozo 案中，EFF 与美国公民自由联盟、其马里兰州、北卡罗来纳州、南卡罗来纳州和弗吉尼亚州的附属机构以及全国刑事辩护律师协会 (NACDL) 一起提交了一份非当事人意见陈述。

我们认为，边境的电子设备搜查应该需要基于可能原因的搜查令，但至少，无论官员是手动搜查还是使用插入设备并下载其全部内容进行搜查的取证软件进行搜查，相同的第四修正案标准都应适用于边境的所有设备搜查。

不幸的是，法院驳回了这一论点，并裁定手动搜查适用较低的标准，从而允许政府在不怀疑有任何不当行为的情况下进行极具侵入性的电子设备搜查，仅仅是因为边境官员选择手动搜查而不是使用法医工具。

边境搜查例外与您的手机相遇

第四修正案要求政府对人员或财产的搜查是合理的，这通常意味着从法官那里获得基于可能原因的搜查令。

但是，如果属于搜查令要求的例外情况，包括允许官员在边境搜查您的物品的例外情况，则无证搜查仍然是合理的。 考虑到政府在控制谁和什么可以进入该国方面的利益，边境搜查例外允许在无授权的情况下搜查穿越美国边境的人员或财产，包括国际机场等边境的功能等同物。

从历史上看，法院将行李、车辆和个人物品的边境搜查归为“例行公事”，因此即使在没有怀疑旅行者有不当行为的情况下进行，也是合理的；法院还认为，更具侵入性的“非常规”搜查，例如某些身体搜查和损坏财产的搜查，需要合理怀疑。

但一个人对手机或笔记本电脑上的个人数据的隐私利益与他们对手提箱内容的有限隐私利益截然不同。

最高法院在 Riley v. 案中讨论了手机隐私问题。 加利福尼亚州（2014）认为，搜查令要求的逮捕例外不适用于手机，因此通常需要搜查手机的搜查令，至少在该国内部是如此。 法院认识到人们在手机中拥有前所未有的隐私利益，以及即使是简短的手动搜索也可以揭示“个人私生活的全部内容”，包括我们的政治立场、宗教信仰、性取向等。 因此，最高法院认为，由于电子设备搜查与袋子或物理容器的搜查“几乎没有相似之处”，因此应对它们进行不同的评估。

继莱利之后，第四巡回法院考虑了两个涉及法医搜索的边境设备搜查案件，其中边境官员使用外部软件提取和分析设备的数据。

在美国诉。 Kolsuz (2018)，第四巡回法院认为，在边境对手机进行法证搜查“必须被视为非常规边境搜查，需要对跨国犯罪采取某种程度的个人怀疑”，但法院拒绝决定该标准是否只是合理怀疑，还是合理怀疑。

然后在美国诉。 Aigbekaen (2019)，第四巡回法院认为，为支持纯粹的国内执法调查而在边境进行法证设备搜查需要搜查令。 法院还重申了科尔苏兹关于边境相关设备法医搜查的一般规则：“政府必须对与边境搜查例外的保护国家安全、征收关税、阻止不受欢迎人员入境或扰乱进出口违禁品的目的有某种联系的犯罪行为进行个别怀疑。”

在贝尔蒙特卡多佐，人工搜查终于在法庭上进行。

令人失望的决定

当何塞·贝尔蒙特·卡多佐从玻利维亚前往美国时，他已经受到美国政府的关注，并在华盛顿杜勒斯国际机场受到了美国海关和边境保护局 (CBP) 官员的接见。 该警官手动搜查了他的手机，发现了被视为“数字违禁品”的儿童性虐待材料 (CSAM)，导致贝尔蒙特·卡多佐被捕并受到刑事起诉。

上诉的争议焦点是边境手动设备搜查应适用什么标准。 第四巡回法院认为，与法医搜查不同，人工搜查是“例行公事”，因此根据第四修正案是合理的，无需搜查令或个人怀疑。

法院的判决取决于手动搜查和法医搜查之间的四个区别：（1）在手动搜查中，是人进行搜索，而不是机器； (2) 人工搜查的广度取决于官员的时间和精力，而法医搜查则涉及全面； (3) 手动搜索仅揭示用户通常可以访问的内容，而取证搜索可以发现已删除的文件、缓存的片段、元数据等； (4) 手动搜查会受到警官记忆衰退或记录不完善的影响，而法医搜查会创建永久副本。

但在识别这些技术差异时，法院从未解释为什么他们证明较低的手动搜索标准是合理的。

第四巡回法院的判决是有问题的，因为正如我们在法庭之友陈述中所说，手动搜索所涉及的数据类别与法医搜索相同，这些数据可以揭示我们身份和生活的高度个人化方面。 搜查是通过特工的拇指还是通过软件进行并不重要：最终结果同样具有侵入性，因此所有设备搜查都应符合搜查令要求，或至少符合第四修正案的标准。

法院反复强调，这里的搜查只持续了两分钟，表明限时搜查并不侵犯隐私。 但个人对其个人数据的隐私利益不会根据手机被搜索的方式或时间长短而改变。 滚动浏览某人的个人短信或照片两分钟是对隐私的侵犯，即使在这么短的时间内也可能会泄露该人的私密细节。

此外，随着设备原生搜索功能的改进，手动搜索可以通过关键字搜索在几秒钟内显示个人信息，即使是照片，可能需要滚动一个小时才能找到相同的信息，这进一步表明限时搜索不一定会减少对隐私的侵犯。 重要的不是搜索本身的广度，而是我们手机上前所未有的（并且不断增长的）数据广度。

一线希望

还有一线希望：第四巡回法院依靠搜查持续了两分钟的事实，留下了一种可能性，即更长的手动搜查可能会引发更高的怀疑要求。 但在划定明确界限之前，第四巡回法院管辖范围内的边境官员可以使用手动搜查来避开本来适用的更高的第四修正案标准。 与此同时，EFF 将继续打击边境上未经授权、毫无嫌疑的极具侵入性的设备窃听行为，并制定严格的隐私标准来保护我们最个人的数据。
